item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image has been removed due to not being compliant with CC BY-SA 3.0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's with the decimal numbering of subjects?

SCP-2200-2.037

SCP-2200-2.082

SCP-2200-4.00581

It's kind of jarring and distracts from the main story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How have there been 84 hosts? Does it change hosts sometimes even when you do the containment procedures? Or maybe there has been a LOT of human error resulting in many containment failures. Or possibly there was a period at the start when containment procedures weren't fully figured out. And I guess the number of guys that get killed by police before being contained divides this number, but it is probably a large number still. I assume the 2200-2.084 means it is the 84th -2.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I only wonder, how SCP-2200 will work after ΩK?

item.title: Re: Lucky You
item.content:encoded:
Aaand it's up:

https://youtu.be/1NfAvqpt8_w

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would like to apologize to the Volgun and other SCP readers (and translators!) for the numerous of tweaks and edits this one has gone through since the Bung animation was announced. It was originally written as a bit of an in-joke, and I'll admit it's not my best work, so I felt a shade of terror at the thought that, for many people, this would be the first—and possibly only—SCP of mine they'd read. It's too late for heavy rewrites at this point, so I figured I might as well tidy up what's here as best I can.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, err, there's a good reason for that, y'see: I dun goofed. Take a look at the discussion pages for pretty much any SCP I've written and you'll find that I have never, ever been able to get my numbers straight. I really ought to start blackboxing all of it, honestly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article states that all instances of SCP-2200-2 are between 16-34 years of age, yet the first interviewee is 36 years old. Have I missed something? Was he perhaps 34 at the time of receiving 2200-1 and this interview takes place 2 years later?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can’t believe I never noticed that! Thanks for pointing it out; it's now been fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
after SCP-2200-2.037 was detained.

This phrase is mentioned in interviews that SCP-2200-2.037 did not take part in. Is that intentional or did you forget to change the numbers?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great, but I didn't really like how the subjects in the interviews basically flat out state the theme of the article, rather than leaving it up to the reader for interpretation.

item.title: Lucky You
item.content:encoded: According to a trailer released today, this SCP is getting the Lord Bung treatment!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Made some (long overdue) housekeeping edits to this one. The biggest change is that it's now Keter. Heck, the Foundation has to "feed" a Class-D to it every month, and even that isn't a guarantee it won't teleport elsewhere and cause another killing spree halfway across the world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2200-4 seem to be innately aware of the dimensions of the "safe" area. Instances that leave the area remain permanently inert and cannot be restored by being returned to SCP-2200-4.

Shouldn't that be "SCP-2200-3" at the end? SCP-2200-3 is the city area.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So was it a random stretch of land before the first people arrived there?
How did a city start up? Where did they get the resources for building if they are constrained by space?
Can they die from causes other than leaving the area of effect? Are they SOLID silver? Are they real silver?
Does the silveration of the sword holder turn them into a -4 eventually?

But I like the idea of a religion springing up in a colony of dead-but-not people, and that makes the skip work overall for me. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Found a couple things I'm pretty sure are mistakes:

Instances that leave the area remain permanently inert and cannot be restored by being returned to SCP-2200-4.

Fairly certain this should refer to -3.

Your life will only end when you are willing cross the city limits.

And I think this should be "willing to cross".

That said, even without being aware of the in-joke, I really liked this. I do wish there had been more details on what life is actually like in Soulberg, since that was the most interesting part to me personally. Still good enough to upvote though, so +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was unaware of the joke prior to reading the comments, but I still found the latter part of the article fascinating and well written. The entire part about a knife which makes you glow and want to kill people felt odd without context. The knife and Soulberg could be two entirely different articles. Everything about the knife felt pretty clichéd, but obviously after finding out what this was based on that makes a lot of sense, and I appreciated what the author was able to do here more.
I'd honestly love to see the idea of Soulberg and people that live there explored further, so +1 just for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't know what the joke was, and I wasn't interested with the article at the start, but the interviews made it interesting and worth the read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What would happen if they killed themselves with the sword by some means of, accident, suicide, blob, etc… would they be sent to Soulberg or would they just die? here is a +1.

E: i also noticed [[>]] <- these around the rating bar. its fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had the reaction to the reference that you were going for, but ultimately the piece feels unfocused. I think it would be better if it focused more on the soul city and the knife, and the story behind or in front of them. I don't really care about some schmuck's fears of death, or about them turning blue, or about the knife imprinting on people or whatever. There are far simpler ways to get a particular knife to be used to kill people, and they would take up less text.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting, I wonder how far the "fear of death" extends considering that they ask for pets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought this was pretty interesting, even without knowledge of the in-joke. On the other hand, SCP-2200-1 is referred to as a "sword" at the beginning, and later as a "knife". Also, it's referred to as "possibly independently intelligent to some degree", which I find breaks the tone a little. Maybe "possibly sentient to some degree"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So basically an author is never allowed to take inspiration from a bad idea.

That's not what I said. If you want to discuss this, don't put words in my mouth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It isn't that authors aren't allowed to take failed SCP ideas and make them work…that's basically what a rewrite is. It's that in-jokes and scips making fun of bad scips rarely do well…and can be construed as rude towards the original author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So basically an author is never allowed to take inspiration from a bad idea.

Like, who cares if the origin of the article is some old bad one, the current one is interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know what a Soulberg is, but this is a neat collection of weird effects that play off each other fairly well. I particularly like the contents of the final interview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In Pepper's defense I had forgotten about the original Soulberg and thought this article raised some interesting questions. I don't think that injoke scps are inherently bad so long as they can be enjoyed without the requisite knowledge1, though I will admit that immortalizing the sins of a hapless noobie is a little meanspirited. Then again, this is a lot gentler than a decomm, so take that as you will.

Footnotes
1. In the interest of full disclosure, as the author of SCP-1040 ("Memetic Emu"), I am not impartial on this point

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Soulberg was a bad article posted probably about a year ago featuring a Super Saiyan Gary Stu with a giant sword and a blue aura that got bigger as he got angrier or something. His name was actually Soulberg.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, what's going on here? Was this based off a now-gone article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dislike in-jokes being made into SCPs. It encourages people to coast with subpar articles because the "OMG someone actually did that!1!" reaction tends to be the most salient part of the piece, rather than any inherent interest or storytelling. It also makes the SCP experience more insular for newer users and thus limits our audience.

Additionally, I dislike permanent memorials to bad articles so they can be made fun of forever. This place is harsh and unsparing in its criticism, as it should be. But stuff like this crosses the line into petty meanness and contempt, IMO.

This particular article lacks any sort of interest to me. It's an overly complex version of random thing what does a random thing. I guess there's a "is the copy really the person" kind of thing going on in there, but it could have been handled far better without the extraneous goofiness. So really, this is pretty much riding the Soulberg thing almost entirely. For the reasons outlined above, that engenders my emphatic downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Were you around for Soulberg, PG?

Also, it's just barely passable for me. I'm impressed you explained the core of the original article, but I was very impassive towards the actual ideas presented until I got to the interview logs, which collectively won me over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved the picture, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oddly enough, I'm not fond. It just…doesn't feel right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get the in-joke but I found this enjoyable. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It happened. Wiki over.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I knew this was coming and I still audibly swore when I realized what this was.

+1

item.title: New SCP for Series III?
item.content:encoded:
Why not Soulberg?

I'm going to regret this I know I am

EDIT: Four years later, I only half regret it. Check back in 2022 for further updates.

Discover more of my weird ramblings in
~PeppersGhost's Gallery of Illusion!~
